level-1/card01:
My role: I am a [your role - e.g., sales executive / software engineer / investor / job seeker / journalist / procurement manager].
Company to research: [COMPANY NAME]
Business group or division of interest: [BUSINESS GROUP / DIVISION NAME - e.g., Cloud Services, Financial Services, Healthcare IT, Digital Transformation, or "All" if you want the full company]
Research this company thoroughly and produce a structured profile that is useful to both a business decision-maker AND a technical evaluator. If a specific business group or division is mentioned above, focus the research primarily on that group while still providing company-level context. If "All" is specified, cover the full company. In either case, include a dedicated section mapping out all known business groups, divisions, and subsidiaries - and highlight any that are related to or overlap with the specified group of interest. Use plain language for business sections. Use precise technical language where appropriate in tech sections but always include a one-line plain-English summary for each technical finding. Cite sources and flag anything unverifiable or older than 12 months.
SECTION A - BUSINESS PROFILE (accessible to everyone)
A1. Identity & Basics Full legal name, headquarters, year founded, founder(s), current CEO. Entity type (public / private / subsidiary / non-profit). Stock ticker and exchange if public. One-paragraph origin story. Mission or vision statement in their own words.
A2. Business Group Map List all known business groups, divisions, business units, subsidiaries, and acquired brands within the company. For each, provide: name, what it does (one line), estimated size or revenue contribution if known, and current group head or president. If a specific business group was specified above, clearly mark which groups are directly related, adjacent, or share customers/technology with the group of interest. Present this as a structured list or table.
A3. What They Sell (in plain language) Core products and services - explain each as if to someone with zero industry knowledge. If a specific business group is specified, lead with that group's products first, then briefly cover the rest. Who buys from them (consumer, enterprise, SMB, government)? What specific pain point does each product solve? Any major product launches, discontinuations, or pivots in the last 24 months.
A4. Business Model & Revenue How do they make money (subscription, licensing, ads, services, hardware, marketplace, etc.)? Revenue breakdown by segment, business group, or geography if available. If a specific group is specified, isolate that group's revenue contribution and growth trend where possible. Pricing model and typical deal size if known.
A5. Financials & Funding If public: latest annual revenue, net income/loss, gross margin, market cap, YoY revenue growth for last 3 years. If private: total funding raised, last valuation, most recent round (date, size, lead investors), known runway or profitability status. If a specific group is specified, include any segment-level financial data reported for that group. Key takeaway: are they financially healthy, growing, stagnant, or distressed?
A6. Market Position & Competitive Landscape Top 5 direct competitors - at the company level and, if a specific group is specified, at the business group level separately. Estimated market share or ranking. What differentiates them - their moat. Any Gartner Magic Quadrant, Forrester Wave, G2, or similar analyst placements. Include a simple comparison table: Company vs. top 3 competitors across price, key feature, target customer, and market perception.
A7. Customers & Social Proof Named enterprise customers or case studies. If a specific group is specified, prioritize customers of that group. Notable logos on their website. Any published customer count or ARR milestones. NPS or customer satisfaction scores if available. Awards or certifications (ISO, SOC2, industry-specific).
A8. Leadership & People Key C-suite bios (2-3 lines each). If a specific group is specified, also include that group's leadership (president, VP, GM). Board members or notable advisors. Approximate headcount and 12-month trend (hiring, freezing, or laying off) - at company level and group level if available. Glassdoor rating and one-line sentiment summary. Notable recent hires or departures and why they matter.
A9. Recent News (last 12 months) The 5-7 most significant events in reverse chronological order. If a specific group is specified, include news specific to that group in addition to major company-wide events. Cover: product launches, partnerships, acquisitions, funding rounds, leadership changes, lawsuits, regulatory actions, or PR incidents. One-sentence summary per event with date.
A10. Risks & Red Flags Known controversies, lawsuits, data breaches, regulatory investigations, or executive scandals. Analyst downgrades. Negative press patterns. Customer complaint trends. Concentration risk (single customer, single market, single product). If a specific group is specified, flag risks unique to that group's market or operations.
A11. Future Outlook Analyst consensus or expert predictions. Public roadmap or stated strategy. Expansion into new markets, products, or geographies. If a specific group is specified, include that group's strategic direction and investment signals. Any rumored IPO, M&A, or pivot. Overall trajectory: accelerating, steady, decelerating, or uncertain.
SECTION B - TECHNICAL PROFILE (for engineering and product teams)
B1. Technology Stack Known programming languages, frameworks, databases, cloud providers, and infrastructure tools. If a specific group is specified, identify that group's stack separately if it differs from the parent company. Source this from job postings, engineering blogs, StackShare, GitHub, and conference talks. Provide a one-line plain-English summary.
B2. Product Architecture Monolith vs. microservices. API-first or closed. SaaS, on-premise, hybrid, or self-hosted options. Mobile apps (native or cross-platform). Known scalability or reliability track record (any publicized outages?).
B3. API & Developer Ecosystem Do they offer a public API? Quality of documentation. SDKs and supported languages. Developer community size (GitHub stars, npm downloads, Discord/Slack community). Marketplace or plugin ecosystem.
B4. Open Source & Engineering Culture Notable open-source projects they maintain or contribute to. GitHub org activity (repos, contributors, commit frequency). Engineering blog quality and posting frequency. Presence at tech conferences. Published tech radar or architecture decision records.
B5. Data, AI & Security Posture AI/ML capabilities or initiatives. Data handling practices. Security certifications (SOC2, ISO 27001, GDPR compliance, HIPAA if relevant). Published security policies or bug bounty program. Any known breaches or vulnerabilities.
B6. Technical Hiring Signals Current open engineering roles and what they reveal about priorities. If a specific group is specified, filter for roles within that group. Tech interview process (if publicly known from Glassdoor or blogs). Engineering team size relative to total headcount. Remote vs. on-site engineering culture.
SECTION C - SYNTHESIS
C1. Executive Summary A single paragraph (max 100 words) that a busy decision-maker could read in 30 seconds covering: who they are, what they do, financial health, competitive standing, and one key risk. If a specific group is specified, make this summary about that group in the context of the parent company.
C2. Technical Summary A single paragraph (max 100 words) summarizing their tech maturity, stack modernity, developer-friendliness, and one key technical strength or weakness.
C3. SWOT Table A simple 2x2 grid: Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, Threats - 3 bullet points each. If a specific group is specified, make this SWOT about that group, not the parent company.
C4. Source List All sources used, with URLs and access dates. Flag any source older than 12 months.
level-1/card02: The product launched on January 15 and completed its first 90 days on April 15 with 148,000 total signups against a target of 120,000, exceeding goal by 23.3%. Of the total signups, 94,200 converted to active users giving an activation rate of 63.6%, while 53,800 signed up but never completed onboarding. Daily active users (DAU) averaged 32,400 during month one, grew to 41,800 in month two, and reached 48,600 in month three showing a 50% increase from launch month. Monthly active users (MAU) peaked at 86,300 in month three. The DAU to MAU ratio stands at 56.3% indicating strong daily engagement. Revenue generated in the first 90 days totals $2.14 million, with month one contributing $420,000, month two at $680,000, and month three at $1,040,000. Average revenue per user (ARPU) grew from $4.46 in month one to $12.04 in month three. Paid conversion rate from free to premium is 8.4% against an industry benchmark of 5.2%. Customer acquisition cost (CAC) averaged $12.80 per signup, with paid channels at $18.40 (Google Ads at $16.20, social media ads at $22.10, influencer partnerships at $14.60) and organic channels at $3.20 (SEO at $2.80, referrals at $1.90, word of mouth at $0). By geography, North America accounts for 44.2% of signups, Europe at 28.6%, Asia Pacific at 18.1%, and rest of world at 9.1%. Top feature adoption rates show core dashboard at 92.4%, collaboration tools at 67.8%, AI assistant at 58.3%, integrations marketplace at 41.2%, and advanced analytics at 28.9%. App store rating is 4.6 out of 5 from 12,400 reviews, with 72.3% five-star, 18.1% four-star, 6.2% three-star, and 3.4% below three stars. Support tickets totaled 4,820 in 90 days with average resolution time of 4.2 hours, top issues being login problems at 28%, integration errors at 22%, billing questions at 18%, feature requests at 17%, and bugs at 15%. NPS after 90 days is +52, with promoters at 64.1%, passives at 23.8%, and detractors at 12.1%. Churn in the first 90 days is 6.8% of activated users, with the primary churn reason being missing integrations at 34%, followed by pricing at 26%, complexity at 21%, and switched to competitor at 19%.
level-1/card04:
The "Project Pulse" Architect
Persona: Project Manager · Program Lead · Delivery Manager | Tool: Gemini Enterprise + Google Drive
The Scenario
A Senior PM managing four concurrent workstreams for a critical product launch relies on team leads for updates — but the real ground truth is buried in hours of recorded meeting transcripts sitting in Google Drive. Every Sunday evening, the PM manually wades through Sprint Syncs, Architecture Reviews, and Stakeholder Feedback sessions to piece together a status report for Monday morning.
The Problem
Transcripts are a goldmine — and completely unreadable at scale. At 10,000+ words per hour, a PM reviewing three meetings is staring at 30,000 words of raw dialogue. Critical blockers mentioned in passing — “the API documentation is outdated,” “we’re waiting on the vendor” — get missed until they cause a delay. The PM is permanently reactive, fighting fires that were visible in Tuesday’s transcript.
The AI Use Case
Every Sunday evening, the PM runs one prompt. Gemini reads all three transcript types from Google Drive and produces four deliverables — each building on the one before it.
The Prompt:
"Read all transcripts in Google Drive — Transcript_Sprint_Sync_Week14, Transcript_Architecture_Review_APIGateway, Transcript_StakeholderFeedback_SteeringCommittee — and produce the following: (1) Risk Extraction — scan every transcript for blockers, at-risk milestones, and keywords like ‘delay,’ ‘dependency,’ or ‘blocker,’ for each risk state who raised it, in which meeting, and whether it is resolved or open, flagging any blocker that has appeared across more than one session as recurring and any risk mentioned in passing or with minimising language that could be dismissed without careful reading, (2) Commitment Audit — identify every action item or verbal commitment made by a Team Lead across all sessions, flag any commitment with no follow-up confirmation in a subsequent transcript as Unconfirmed, flag any commitment with no deadline stated as requiring clarification, and flag any resource or decision change that was made without explicit PM approval as a process violation, (3) Action Registry — generate a consolidated table across all transcripts with Owner, Task, Deadline, and Status — marking items as Open, Confirmed, Overdue, or Unconfirmed and deduplicating any action item that appears in more than one session, and (4) Executive Briefing — draft a 5-bullet summary for the Steering Committee covering overall project health with a RAG status, one major win, two critical risks requiring immediate decision, and one people or sentiment signal drawn from tone shifts or late escalation patterns observed across the transcripts."
Knowledge Sources:
Google Drive — Transcript_Sprint_Sync_Week14, Transcript_Architecture_Review_APIGateway, Transcript_StakeholderFeedback_SteeringCommittee
Executive Impact
The Sunday Night Scramble is eliminated. Thirty thousand words of raw meeting dialogue are compressed into a 10-minute briefing before Monday morning. The PM shifts from Document Chaser to Strategic Navigator — catching risks 72 hours before they hit the critical path, surfacing process violations before they compound, and ensuring no verbal commitment ever disappears into an unread transcript.

level-1/card05:
The "Bid Blueprint Accelerator"
Persona: RFP Solution Lead · Bid Manager · Senior Delivery Head | Tool: Gemini Enterprise + Google Drive + Google Search

The Scenario
An RFP Solution Lead managing pre-sales for a large IT services firm receives a complex RFP from a major financial services firm seeking a technology transformation partner. The submission deadline is tight. The RFP contains vendor response questions across multiple workstreams, mandatory technical requirements, vendor qualification criteria, a multi-section proposal structure with weighted evaluation criteria, and a Compliance Requirements Traceability Matrix that must be filled. The firm's methodology documents, case studies, reference architectures, compliance credentials, and bid playbook sit across Google Drive - but there is no quick way to connect what the RFP demands to what the firm already has.
The Problem
Manually parsing a complex financial services RFP - extracting vendor questions, technical requirements, qualification criteria, mapping them against internal knowledge, drafting responses aligned to evaluation weightage, and filling a compliance matrix - takes days across multiple senior people. The output is inconsistent and person-dependent. By the time solutioning begins, a significant chunk of the response window is already consumed on document reading alone.
The AI Use Case
Every time a new RFP lands, the Solution Lead runs one prompt. Gemini reads the full uploaded RFP, searches the knowledge base in Google Drive, and produces four deliverables - each building on the one before it.
The Prompt:
"Read the uploaded RFP in full and search our methodology documents, case studies, reference architectures, compliance credentials, and bid strategy playbook in Google Drive. Produce the following: (1) extract every requirement from the RFP - all vendor response questions from each workstream, all mandatory technical requirements, and all vendor qualification criteria - assign each a unique ID, classify by domain, and tag as Mandatory or Desirable, extracting only what the document actually states, then fill the Compliance Requirements Traceability Matrix by mapping each technical requirement and vendor qualification against our credentials in Google Drive showing Comply, Partially Comply, or Non-Comply with specific evidence and source document reference, (2) draft a first-cut response for each vendor response question by searching our methodology docs, case studies, and reference architectures in Google Drive - each response must name specific proprietary tools, cite a comparable financial services case study with measurable outcomes, and include a paragraph tailored to the RFP client's specific details, (3) draft all proposal sections required by the RFP, prioritising depth by evaluation weightage - sections mapped to higher-weighted criteria get the deepest treatment, financial services experience sections must reference actual case studies, security and compliance sections must cite specific certifications and regulatory capabilities, and remaining sections must pull from our reference templates and team profiles, and (4) flag any requirement, vendor question, or proposal section where our knowledge base has no matching content as RED with a recommended action to close the gap - flag any deadline falling within the near term as CRITICAL."
Knowledge Sources:
Google Drive - company credentials & compliance capabilities, end-to-end methodology & tooling, financial services case studies, reference architectures & templates, bid strategy playbook
Google Search - regulatory compliance context, industry benchmarks
Executive Impact
The Solution Lead walks into the bid kick-off with every requirement extracted, the compliance matrix filled, draft responses to all vendor questions ready, a full proposal aligned to evaluation weightage, and a clear gap report showing what still needs human attention. A problem that used to consume the entire first week - senior people buried in document parsing instead of solutioning - is now reduced to a brief human review. The bid team skips straight to high-value solutioning and differentiation.

group-01/level3/card01:
Using the 'Sprint Backlog' sheet in this workbook, write a VBA macro that does the following:

1. Reads the 'Status' column (column C) and counts tickets for each of these five statuses:
   Done | In Progress | Blocked | Supervisor Review | To Do

2. Creates a new sheet called 'Dashboard' (delete and recreate if it already exists).

3. Writes a summary table in the Dashboard sheet starting at cell A1 with headers:
   Status | Count | Percentage

4. Creates a Pie Chart from that summary table with:
   - Title: "Sprint Status Overview — Cloud Infrastructure Programme"
   - Slice colours: Done = Green (#00B050), In Progress = Blue (#4472C4),
     Blocked = Red (#FF0000), Supervisor Review = Orange (#FFC000), To Do = Grey (#808080)
   - Data labels showing both category name and percentage
   - Chart positioned at cell D1, sized 400 × 300 pixels

5. The macro must be re-runnable: if 'Dashboard' already exists, delete it before recreating.

Using the 'Sprint Backlog' and 'Squad Targets' sheets, write a VBA macro that:

SECTION A — STACKED BAR CHART (Sprint Velocity)
1. From the 'Squad Targets' sheet, aggregate per sprint:
   - Planned Velocity (sum of all 4 squad values)
   - Actual Velocity (sum of all 4 squad values)
   Compute Completion % = Actual / Planned * 100 (round to 1 decimal).

2. Write a Sprint Summary table to Dashboard!A1:D4 with headers:
   Sprint | Planned Velocity (pts) | Actual Velocity (pts) | Completion %

3. Create a Stacked Bar Chart from this table showing Actual (blue) and
   Remaining = Planned - Actual (light grey) stacked to the Planned total.
   Title: "Sprint Velocity — Cloud Infrastructure Programme"
   Position: Dashboard!F1, sized 500 × 280 pixels.

4. In the Sprint Summary table, highlight the Completion % cell RED (#FFC7CE
   fill, #9C0006 font) for any sprint below 70%.

SECTION B — BURNDOWN CHART (Weeks 0–6)
5. From 'Sprint Backlog', calculate remaining points per week end:
   Week 0 = SUM of all Original Points
   Week N = Week (N-1) - SUM of Original Points for Done tickets with Week Number = N

6. Compute the Ideal burndown: Week 0 value / 6, decreasing linearly to 0.

7. Write a Burndown table to Dashboard!A7:C14 with headers:
   Week | Actual Remaining | Ideal Remaining

8. Create a Dual-Line chart from this burndown table.
   Actual line: solid orange. Ideal line: dashed grey.
   Title: "Programme Burndown — Sprints 12–14"
   Position: Dashboard!F11, sized 500 × 280 pixels.

The macro must be re-runnable.

Using all three sheets, write a VBA macro that builds a full Programme Health Dashboard
on a 'Dashboard' sheet (delete and recreate if it already exists). The dashboard must
contain exactly five sections laid out top-to-bottom:

SECTION 1 — SUMMARY BANNER (rows 1–4)
Display 4 KPI cells side by side: Total Tickets (60), % Complete (41.7%),
Active Blockers (9), Sprints At Risk (1 — Sprint 14 only).
Apply a dark navy (#1F2D3D) background with white bold text to each KPI label cell.
Apply a light blue (#DAEEF3) fill to each KPI value cell.

SECTION 2 — SQUAD RADAR CHART (rows 6–26)
Using 'Squad Targets' for the MOST RECENT SPRINT (Sprint 14), calculate 5 metrics
for each of the 4 squads and normalise each metric to a 0–1 scale
(divide by the maximum value across all squads for that metric):
  a) Velocity Score   = Actual Velocity / Planned Velocity
  b) On-Time Score    = Actual On-Time % / On-Time Target %
  c) Blocker Resistance = 1 - (Blocker Count / Max Blockers Allowed)  ← INVERTED
  d) Completion Score = Actual Velocity / MAX(Planned Velocity across all squads)
  e) Health Index     = IF(Health Status = "Green",1, IF("Amber",0.5, 0))
Normalise each metric column so the highest squad = 1.
Create a Radar Chart titled "Squad Health Radar — Sprint 14".
Position at Dashboard!G6, size 420 × 300 pixels.

SECTION 3 — SPRINT SCORECARD (rows 28–34)
Build a scorecard table: Sprint | Overall Completion % | Blocker Count | Health RAG
Colour the Health RAG cell: Green (#C6EFCE) = ≥80%, Amber (#FFEB9C) = 70–79%,
Red (#FFC7CE) = <70%.

SECTION 4 — RISK LOG (rows 36–48)
From 'Sprint Backlog', extract ALL tickets with Status = "Blocked".
Display: Ticket ID | Summary (truncated to 60 chars) | Squad | Sprint | Priority | Blocker Reason
Sort by Sprint descending (Sprint 14 first), then Priority (Critical before High).
Apply a red fill (#FFC7CE) to Critical rows, orange (#FFE0B2) to High rows.

SECTION 5 — CEREMONY INSIGHTS (rows 50–65)
From 'Sprint Ceremonies', filter to Sprint 14 rows ONLY.
Display: Ceremony | Date | Key Decisions / Actions | Risks Raised | Status
Title the section "Sprint 14 Ceremony Insights — Technical Lead Escalations".
Apply amber (#FFEB9C) fill to any row where the Risks Raised cell is not blank.

All section titles must use bold navy text. The macro must be fully re-runnable.
